name: Phase 9 — Arrival Below the Clean Paper

text.content:
Phase 9 — Below the Clean Paper

Alternate title: “The First Under-Road.”

Phase summary

Phase 9 is the first true deep-route phase. The party crosses the Final Threshold Landing beneath Tarnsmere and finds the surface conspiracy's paperwork has a basement — and the basement has been keeping records longer than the surface has been asking questions.

This is not a megadungeon. This is not the full reveal. This is the First Under-Road Waystation: a compact, procedural transfer point where surface ledgers, aliases, tokens, and couriers are converted into route marks, witnessed transfers, and tally cuts. The party should leave Phase 9 understanding that the surface conspiracy did not invent these systems. It learned from them.

Phase flow

  1. Final descent from Tarnsmere through the older stair.
  2. Arrival at the First Under-Road Waystation ("Witness Station Seven" in old route marks).
  3. Initial orientation: tally walls, token cages, and the first route marks.
  4. Evidence of recent transfer — Haskur's or Kethren's passage, route-cleaner activity, or both.
  5. Exploration of waystation systems: account hall, registry wall, route office, supply cache, burn furnace.
  6. Encounter with Route Warden Savax, route-cleaners, the Unclaimed Witness, or remnants.
  7. Recovery of the Witness Tally and Recovery Crate Tag.
  8. Revelation of the Blooming Rot recovery logic.
  9. Opening or bypassing the Black Ledger Gate.
  10. Ending choice at the Lower Route Fork: follow Haskur's transfer, Kethren's route, the supply line, or retreat with evidence.

Sessions expected

One to two sessions of compact, tense site play. The waystation is small. The pressure is bureaucratic.

Player-facing language constraint

This phase introduces the first true deep route. Do not use the word "Underdark" in read-aloud text, handouts, NPC dialogue, or any player-facing explanation. The world below the threshold uses different language. Acceptable substitutes:

  • below
  • deep road, under-road, lower route
  • descent, old stair
  • permitted road, witnessed road
  • the route beneath the roads, the road under roads

GM-facing notes may say what this is in plain terms. Players should hear what the route calls itself.

Tone for the table

This is the moment the party crosses from investigation into trespass. The surface investigation was hostile but legible — people lied, but they lied with paper. Below, the systems are equally legible, but the party is not authorized to read them. The procedure is not theirs.

The final sensation Phase 9 should produce: the surface world kept ledgers. Below, something has been keeping witnesses.

pages.name: GM Truth — What the Waystation Is

text.content:
GM Truth — What the First Under-Road Waystation Is

What it is

The First Under-Road Waystation is a controlled transfer point on an old lower route. It receives, marks, sorts, and moves people, packets, and cargo between surface-facing networks and the deeper route. It is staffed (when staffed) by route wardens, route-cleaners, and silent couriers. It has been operating in some form for centuries. The current Blooming Rot conspiracy uses it as one of many; it is not the conspiracy's headquarters.

What passes through it

  • Instructions — coded directives between surface-facing handlers (V. Korre, Cindren & Vhal residuals, others) and deeper authorities.
  • Samples — including, recently, Blooming Rot crop and tissue samples.
  • Witnesses — living people moved as custody objects to attest that a particular transfer occurred.
  • Identities — surface names converted into route marks; route marks converted back into surface aliases when assets need to be re-inserted.
  • Payment proofs — tokens, tally marks, and witnessed registry entries that record obligation rather than money.
  • Compromised agents — recovered, in route language. Haskur Vandrell was one such recovery, if he was processed here.

The witnessing concept

The route uses witnessing as a formal administrative function. The word does not mean what surface law means by it. Below:

  • A witness can be a person who saw something — but more often, a witness is a record: a mark, a token, a tally cut, or a living custody object whose presence confirms that a transfer was authorized and completed.
  • The route's central question of any party that descends without procedure is: are you witnessed, or are you cargo?
  • Unwitnessed people are cargo. Cargo is moved. Cargo does not require permission. Cargo does not get to ask questions.

Recent activity

The waystation has been disturbed in the last two to four weeks by:

  • Haskur Vandrell's recovery — if the conspiracy retrieved him after his Phase 6 contingency-escape, he passed through here as recoverable cargo, witnessed by Kethren or another courier with escort authority.
  • Kethren Ilvath's passage — he was here recently, possibly multiple times.
  • V. Korre's channel closure — her surface authority ended at this station's outermost office. The waystation has shut her line.
  • The party's surface activity above — route-cleaners have been sweeping records ahead of an anticipated descent.

What the party can learn here

  1. Haskur's surface identity was converted into a route identity. “Andren” appears only on attached surface scraps, scheduled for burning.
  2. Haskur was not the operation's center. His mark is “recoverable but unreliable.”
  3. Kethren outranks Haskur on the route. He is marked “escort authority.”
  4. Blooming Rot was a test of recoverability. “Blooming was proof. Rot was proof. Recovery was the question.”
  5. A deeper authority exists. An unfamiliar mark sits above Korre, Sereth, the Cindren & Vhal residuals, and even Haskur.
  6. The route branches beyond the Black Ledger Gate.

What the party cannot learn yet

  • Who “E.” is. The mark is recognisable; the name is not on file at this station.
  • The name or nature of the deepest authority.
  • Where the route ultimately leads.
  • Why the entire Blooming Rot experiment was ordered.
  • Kethren's species, home, or origin. The route does not record what surface law would call demographics. It records obligations.

What this phase preserves of the campaign's core horror

The party should leave with the conviction that respectable surface institutions have been feeding people and information into a system they pretend does not exist. The Aerdy Commercial-Court Network's old paperwork, the Guild's missing claims, the Brass Crow couriers, the Kestrel & Reed bonded receipts — all of it has been a surface vocabulary for the same procedural machinery the party now sees in carved stone.

Opening Read-Aloud — The Last of the Stair

Read this when the party leaves the Final Threshold Landing beneath Tarnsmere and begins the descent proper. It is route-neutral — works for all six branching openings (A–F). Branch-specific openings on the next pages add or modify after this beat.

The stair widens almost imperceptibly. Above you, somewhere, is the lintel that said “Accounts above. Witness below.” You stopped looking back at it three landings ago. You are not entirely sure why.

The air does not move. Not in the way air ought to move in a stone passage of this size. It does not push back at you when you step into it, and it does not curl away when you pass. It sits. The lamp you carry burns vertically. The smoke rises a hand's breadth above the wick and then hangs, as though resting, before it dissipates.

The stone changes character. The cut blocks above were excellent civic work — precise, mortar-less, the kind of stone you have seen in Aerdy commercial halls and the older quarters of Greyhawk City. Here, below, the cuts are different. Not worse. Older. The blocks are joined by something that is not mortar and not flux, something that has aged into a thin black seam. The seams catch the lamplight strangely; they are not glossy, but they are not dull either. They look as though they were poured.

You count your landings. Seven so far below the threshold. The eighth is wider still; the stair flattens into a long, low chamber whose far wall is barely visible in your lamp's reach.

On the wall beside the eighth landing, at chest height, there is no inscription. There are tally marks. Six of them, vertically, in three groups of two, with a small horizontal cut crossing the bottom of each pair. You do not recognise the system. You do recognise that it is a system. Whoever cut these knew the marks would be read.

You hear water. Somewhere below and to the left, slow water moving over old stone. The sound is the first thing in many minutes that is not your own breathing.

From the dark ahead of you, no challenge. No call. No movement.

The stair does not feel abandoned. It feels unstaffed.

Beats to land at the table

  • The phrase “No sail. No road.” from the Phase 7 Ashen Itinerary should be lingering in the party's mind here. If anyone says it aloud, let it land without comment. The route is the third option that phrase implied.
  • The smoke hanging is the first concrete environmental signal that this is not surface space. Players should pick up that something about the air is different.
  • The shift in masonry — surface civic stone to older stone — is the first concrete signal that what is below predates what is above. Phase 8 said this in words (Eft's journal, the lintel inscription); here it is shown in the stone.
  • “Unstaffed” is the key word. It is not abandoned. It is not safe. Someone runs this place. They are not here this minute.

Opening A — Clean Entry

Trigger: The party opened the Permission Door in Phase 8 cleanly — Method A (token), B (registry), C (Kethren), D (Korre papers with listening tube disabled), or Elle's iron key. Lockdown Clock 0–2 at descent.

State of the waystation

The waystation has not been fully alerted. The route-cleaners scrubbing surface records did not get a route-bell signal from above; they had reason to expect a descent, but no reason to expect this descent at this hour.

What the party finds in the first chambers

The first chambers below the eighth landing are dim, intact, and watchful. The lamps are protected in deep niches and they have been recently re-oiled; no one has put them out, but no one has been here in the last hour.

You hear no voices. You hear, faintly, the soft rhythmic scratching of a tool on stone — somewhere in the chambers ahead of you, someone is working without speaking.

The tally marks on the wall beside the eighth landing extend, you now see, all the way along the left-hand wall. Hundreds of marks. Some old enough that the cuts are softened by handling. Some so fresh the chips are still in the floor below them.

Initial advantage

The party can observe before being observed. They have one to two scenes' worth of unalerted exploration before the waystation registers their presence. They can:

  • Approach the Account Hall (Location 2) and read tally marks without being challenged.
  • Find the working scribe (one route-cleaner, in the Burn Furnace, Location 8, destroying surface paper) and choose how to engage.
  • Recover the Witness Tally Fragment (Location 3) before it is removed.

Clocks at start

  • Under-Road Alert Clock: 0.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 1 (route-cleaners are already working on surface paper in the Burn Furnace).
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0.

Suggested opening beat

Let the party hear the scratching of the route-cleaner's tool on stone for a long moment before they decide what to do. Reward stealth. Reward observation. The first PC who makes a Perception check passive 13 or above hears it; describe it for them in private if at the table, or in a whisper-chat in VTT. Let them choose whether to share it.

Opening B — Forced Entry

Trigger: The party forced the Permission Door in Phase 8 (Method E), or used Knock-with-malformed-phrase, or otherwise made structural noise crossing the threshold. Structural Failure Clock 2+ at descent.

State of the waystation

The wardens below heard stone work above several hours ago. They have had time to partly prepare. Not fully — the route-bell signal that came down was ambiguous, and the deeper authority has not yet been formally notified. But the inner shutters are closing, the Account Hall lamps are being damped, and a route-cleaner team has begun an emergency record sweep.

What the party finds in the first chambers

The first chambers are partly sealed. A grate of dark iron has descended across the corridor leading into the main hall ahead; you can see through it but you cannot easily pass without lifting it. The grate has a manual winch on the far side, well out of reach.

You hear, distinctly now, the sound of stone scraping on stone — not your own footfalls, but tally plates being lifted from the wall and stacked on a stone floor. Whoever is doing this is not hurrying. They are not slow, either. They are working at the pace of a person who has done this before.

From somewhere deeper, a single sharp metallic tone — like a bell rim being touched once with a knuckle. A signal. It is answered, after a count of seven, by a second tone from a different direction.

Initial complication

The party must get through the grate (Athletics DC 16 to lift one corner, Sleight of Hand or Thieves' Tools DC 15 on the lock mechanism, or Investigation DC 13 finds an unlocked maintenance crawl beside the grate that requires a Medium creature to remove gear), and they must do so knowing the wardens have started counting.

Clocks at start

  • Under-Road Alert Clock: 2 (Distant signal knock; inner shutters closing).
  • Record Destruction Clock: 2 (Surface-name slips burning; witness tokens being removed).
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 1 (the noise above has loosened some upper supports).

Suggested opening beat

The two answering bell tones — the seven-count between them — is a route signal. Eft's journal mentioned route signals. A PC who explicitly recalls Eft (or a History DC 13) recognises: this is a count-and-confirm signal. The wardens are calling in count, not raising a full alarm. The party still has options that don't end in combat.

Opening C — Enemy Ahead

Trigger: Phase 8 Lockdown Clock reached 4 or 5 with the party still descending. Korre's network knew the party was coming and had time to send route-cleaners ahead through a different access (the Cistern Drain bypass or a deeper Greyhawk-end entry the party never discovered).

State of the waystation

Route-cleaners reached the waystation hours, possibly a day, before the party. They are actively burning records in the Burn Furnace. They are removing witness tokens from the Toll-Token Cage. They are scraping the H.V.A. line from the Witness Registry Wall right now. If the party arrives at Opening C, they are arriving during the sweep.

If Haskur was at the waystation as a recovered asset, he may already have been moved on through the Black Ledger Gate — or he may have been judged a liability and quietly killed in the Sealed Witness Cell.

What the party finds in the first chambers

The first chambers are dimly lit by a single working lamp. The other lamps have been struck out. The lamp that remains is set on the floor beside the long stone counter of what looks like a public records office — if a public records office had been carved out of black stone, lit from the floor, and worked at by figures who do not speak.

Three figures stand at the counter. They are in grey, with leather aprons and short scraping tools. One of them is at a wall to your right, lifting a stone tally plate off iron hooks. Another is at a low brazier, feeding a strip of pale parchment into red coals; the parchment crisps and curls. The third has a chisel against the wall ahead of you and is working a vertical row of marks into smooth, blank, unreadable strokes.

None of them have looked up when you arrived. They have heard you. They will look up when they finish what they are doing.

Initial pressure

The party is in a race against destruction. They can:

  • Engage immediately and stop the sweep (Encounter: 3 Route-Cleaners, 1 Silent Courier monitoring from the Warden Post slit).
  • Try to disable the cleaners non-violently (Stealth, Persuasion, etc.) — difficult, but possible.
  • Salvage what they can while the sweep continues, accepting that some evidence will be lost.
  • Locate Savax (in the Old Route Office, Location 5) and treat him as the decision-maker rather than the cleaners.

Clocks at start

  • Under-Road Alert Clock: 3 (Inner shutters closed; route marks being covered; warden team mobilizing).
  • Record Destruction Clock: 3 (Surface-name slips burning; witness tokens removed; H.V.A. line being scraped right now).
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0.

Suggested opening beat

The cleaners do not stop when the party arrives. This is the most important detail. The cleaners finish what they are doing, then look up. If the party watches in silence for a full ten seconds at the table, describe one piece of evidence being destroyed in front of them. Make the party feel the cost of hesitation. Then let them act.

pages.name: Opening D — Captured NPC Guides Entry

text.content:
Opening D — Captured NPC Guides Entry

Trigger: Haskur, Kethren, V. Korre, or another captured Phase 6–8 NPC is alive and with the party as they descend. The NPC used their knowledge of the threshold to ease passage through Phase 8 (Method C: Kethren channel; Method D: Korre papers; or a similar Haskur-led bypass).

State of the waystation

Depends on which NPC. The captured NPC is now a live complication rather than a solution. They know the rooms but not the current personnel. They know the procedure but their authority may be partly or wholly compromised.

If Kethren is the guide

Kethren can name marks. He cannot promise the wardens will recognise his authority once they see him under guard. He may try to escape into a side passage at the first opportunity. He may also be genuinely useful for reading the Witness Registry Wall.

Kethren stops at the eighth landing. He puts his palm flat against the tally marks on the wall, briefly. He withdraws it. He does not look at any of you.

“You should let me walk three paces ahead,” he says, very quietly. “The wardens here will count us by foot-strike. If you all walk in a clump they will count you as one party. If I walk ahead, they will count us as escort plus four cargo. Cargo gets challenged. Escort plus four gets a different kind of challenge.”

He pauses. He has not turned around.

“That is the only advice I will give freely. The rest will cost you.”

If Haskur is the guide

Haskur is afraid. He recognises the air. He does not want to be here. He may panic, freeze, or try to negotiate with the wardens on the party's behalf in ways that betray his unreliability.

Haskur has stopped breathing for a count of three. He resumes, sharply.

“I was here,” he says. “Not by my choice. I do not know where they took me afterward. I do not know if I will be recognised. I do not know if recognition is good for me.”

He looks at the eldest of you. “If I am called out as a recovered asset, they may try to take me. From you. They may consider me theirs.”

If Korre is the guide

Korre is out of her jurisdiction and visibly knows it. Her papers do not work here. She can speak the language of conversion but not the language of permission.

Korre stands at the eighth landing and reads the tally marks slowly. Her face does not move. After a moment she says, without looking at you, “My office ended above the first tally wall. I have never been below it. You should understand that before you bring me one step further.”

Initial complication

The captured NPC must be managed throughout Phase 9. They will try to change the party's category at the Black Ledger Gate (Kethren as escort, Korre as surface witness, Haskur as recovered asset) — useful, but each comes with leverage costs.

Clocks at start

  • Under-Road Alert Clock: 0 if Kethren is leading (his presence reads as expected escort); 1 if Korre; 2 if Haskur.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 1 (route-cleaners working in background).
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0.

Opening E — Threshold Damaged

Trigger: Phase 8 Structural Failure Clock reached 5 or 6, or a major collapse occurred during or just after the party's descent. The stair behind them is partly damaged.

State of the waystation

The waystation itself is intact but the route between the surface and the waystation is now structurally unsound. The wardens know this. They have begun emergency closure procedure — preparing to seal the upper stair, evacuate sensitive records past the Black Ledger Gate, and let the Tarnsmere threshold collapse rather than try to repair it.

The party has descended into a place that is in the middle of being abandoned. Their retreat is uncertain: a Strength (Athletics) DC 16 check or a successful Dexterity (Stealth) DC 14 to slip past wardens working on collapse-prep is required to climb back up the stair safely.

What the party finds in the first chambers

You can hear, faintly behind you, the slow grumble of stone settling. Dust drifts down the stair from above in fine pale streams. The bottom landing has a thin coat of grit on it that was not here when you came down.

The first chambers below are bustling. Quietly bustling. Six figures in grey are moving with purpose — two stacking stone tally plates into reinforced crates, two carrying small leather satchels of records toward the dark passage at the far end of the chamber, two working with mason's tools at the door frame between this chamber and the next, fitting shaped stones into recesses you did not have time to look for.

None of them are alarmed. They are working a schedule. They have a deadline that is not you.

One of them — older, with a leather case at the belt and gloved hands — looks up at your arrival. He does not stop what he is doing. He says, in the precise diction of a clerk addressing a misdirected delivery, “You should not be here. You may be here briefly. If you stay long, you will be considered cargo.”

That is Route Warden Savax.

Initial pressure

The party can:

  • Negotiate. Savax is willing to converse if the party meets him in route language. He is not friendly. He is busy. He will not waste many words.
  • Try to delay the closure. If they damage the collapse-prep setup, the upper stair stays open longer — but the Alert Clock advances rapidly.
  • Race the closure. They have, by the GM's count, perhaps three to four scenes of in-fiction time before the waystation is fully evacuated and the upper stair sealed for good.

Clocks at start

  • Under-Road Alert Clock: 4 (Warden team mobilized).
  • Record Destruction Clock: 2.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 3 (Old supports already groaning).

Opening F — Returned After Refusing

Trigger: The party chose Outcome F (Held at the Threshold) at the end of Phase 8 — they did not descend immediately, and have come back to Tarnsmere days, weeks, or months later, with better preparation, better evidence, or better company.

State of the route above

Korre's network has reacted to the party's refusal. The threshold has been resealed with deeper care than before. The Permission Door's mechanism has been reset and may have been re-keyed. The route-cleaner presence on the surface has thickened. The cairn has been rebuilt with the seven cuts re-blackened and re-counted — a route warden came up to do it personally.

The party will need to re-traverse Phase 8's surface site, but the door may now reject one or more of their previous methods. Decide as GM which methods are still viable; preserve fail-forward.

State of the waystation

The waystation itself is in a state of deferred routine. The wardens know someone tried to get in and didn't. They are not on full alert, but they are watching for the return. Records that were going to be destroyed have been destroyed already. The Burn Furnace has been swept clean and re-banked.

However, the lapsed time has costs for the conspiracy too: route-cleaners have been pulled away to other sites in the meantime, witness tokens have been reorganised, and Haskur (if alive and recovered) has been moved further onward through the Black Ledger Gate.

What the party finds in the first chambers

The waystation is quieter than you expected. You braced for an ambush, for a sealed grate, for warden teams in the doorway. There is none of that. The first chambers are dim, in good order, and almost too neat — as though someone tidied them yesterday in case of a visit they did not actually expect.

The Account Hall's tally walls have been read recently. The fresh marks are gone from the registry — not destroyed, simply completed and filed. Everything that was urgent two months ago is now resolved.

You feel, walking through it, that you have arrived after the funeral.

Initial state

The party has most of the same opportunities as Opening A, with two costs:

  • Haskur's H.V.A. transfer line has been formally filed and most surface-name evidence has been destroyed in scheduled disposal. Recovery is partial only.
  • The Black Ledger Gate has been reset with a slightly different procedure. Methods that worked from prior intelligence (e.g. learned phrases) may need adjustment.

One benefit: the party may have brought along a surface-side ally (Iren Velash, Aldea Veren, Caelith via written instruction, or a Guild expert) whose interpretive help is now real. The first session of Phase 9 can include a moment where that ally reads a mark the party would have missed.

Clocks at start

  • Under-Road Alert Clock: 1.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 4 (most of what would have been destroyed already has been).
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0.

Caelith Dunivar's Last Surface Note (Handout 10)

Optional pre-descent handout, given to the party either at the end of Phase 8 or as they begin Phase 9. It is the last direct communication they will have from Caelith until they return to the surface. If they do not return, the note may be the last words they have from him at all.

Reproduction

Friends —

You will be below by the time you read this. I will not be with you. I would be, if standing on a stair were enough.

I want to tell you three things in the order they need to be heard.

First: do not assume the absence of law means the absence of rules. Every system that has ever moved people in the dark has had rules — written down, sometimes, but more often cut into the walls. The walls below will be cut. Read what is cut. The walls are the documentation.

Second: the people you will meet down there will recognise some of you as cargo. This is not a metaphor. They will not be unkind about it. They will not be cruel. They will simply categorise you, the way a customs officer categorises a sealed crate, and then they will administer their categorisation. You should know this so it does not surprise you. You should also know that you can change the category by speaking the right words. If you do not know the right words, listen for them.

Third: I will be in the city, holding the line. I have arranged, against my better judgment and a Council I do not entirely trust anymore, that if any of you return alive, with any piece of paper or stone or cloth from below, that evidence will be received and held in a place I personally guarantee. I cannot guarantee much else. I can guarantee that.

If you do not return — speak your names aloud, once, in the silence before you begin. I will not hear you. The route will. It records names whether you offer them or not. It will record yours either way. You may as well say them on your terms.

I am sorry I cannot come with you. I am here. I am still here.

— Caelith Dunivar

GM notes

  • This note is the only thing the party carries below that has been written by a surface-world person who knows what they are walking into. It is the last surface voice they will hear.
  • The line “Read what is cut. The walls are the documentation.” is a direct instruction for how to play Phase 9. The party will earn evidence by reading tally walls, not by interrogating people.
  • The line “You can change the category by speaking the right words” is the foreshadowing for the Black Ledger Gate's False Category Method (Method E).
  • If the party speaks their names aloud at the eighth landing as Caelith suggests, the GM should note this for Phase 10. The route does record them. What it does with the record is for Phase 10 to reveal.
